name: install
context: Install, update, and remove npm packages — manages dependencies, lockfiles, peer deps, overrides, and security audits
info: This sub-skill covers the full npm dependency installation lifecycle: `npm install` for adding packages, `npm ci` for clean reproducible installs in CI, `npm update` for upgrading within semver ranges, and `npm uninstall` for removal. It explains package-lock.json management, the flattened node_modules resolution algorithm, `--save-dev` and `--save-exact` flags, peer dependency resolution, optional dependencies, the overrides field for forcing transitive dependency versions, and `npm audit` for vulnerability scanning. Agents use this skill whenever a project needs new packages, when CI pipelines require deterministic builds, or when dependency conflicts need resolution. See [[node/npm]] for the overall npm ecosystem and [[node/npm/scripts]] for running build scripts after installation.
body:
# npm install

Install, update, and remove packages from your Node.js project.

## Install Modes

```bash
# Install all dependencies from package.json
npm install

# Add a production dependency
npm install express

# Add a dev dependency
npm install -D typescript
npm install --save-dev eslint

# Add an exact version (no ^ prefix)
npm install --save-exact lodash@4.17.21
npm install -E lodash@4.17.21

# Add an optional dependency
npm install --save-optional fsevents

# Install globally
npm install -g typescript

# Install from a git repo
npm install github:user/repo
npm install git+https://github.com/user/repo.git#branch

# Install from a tarball or local folder
npm install ./my-local-package
npm install https://example.com/package.tgz

# Install a specific version or range
npm install express@4
npm install express@^4.18.0
npm install express@latest
```

### Install Flags Reference

| Flag | Short | Effect |
|------|-------|--------|
| `--save-dev` | `-D` | Add to devDependencies |
| `--save-exact` | `-E` | Pin exact version (no `^` prefix) |
| `--save-optional` | `-O` | Add to optionalDependencies |
| `--no-save` | | Install without modifying package.json |
| `--global` | `-g` | Install globally |
| `--legacy-peer-deps` | | Ignore peer dependency conflicts |
| `--force` | `-f` | Force install despite conflicts |
| `--omit=dev` | | Skip devDependencies |
| `--dry-run` | | Show what would be installed |

## npm ci — Clean Install for CI

```bash
# Deletes node_modules, installs exactly from package-lock.json
npm ci

# Production only (skip devDependencies)
npm ci --omit=dev
```

`npm ci` is faster and stricter than `npm install`:
- Requires `package-lock.json` to exist
- Deletes `node_modules` before installing
- Never modifies `package.json` or `package-lock.json`
- Fails if lock file is out of sync with `package.json`

| Behavior | `npm install` | `npm ci` |
|----------|---------------|----------|
| Modifies lock file | Yes, if needed | Never |
| Modifies package.json | Yes, if needed | Never |
| Deletes node_modules | No | Yes |
| Requires lock file | No | Yes |
| Fails on mismatch | No | Yes |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |

**Rule**: always use `npm ci` in CI pipelines and Docker builds.

## Updating and Removing

```bash
# Update all packages within semver ranges
npm update

# Update a specific package
npm update express

# Check for outdated packages
npm outdated

# Remove a package
npm uninstall express
npm uninstall -D typescript

# Remove extraneous packages not in package.json
npm prune

# Production prune (remove devDependencies)
npm prune --omit=dev
```

## Peer Dependencies

Peer dependencies declare that your package requires a specific package to be provided by the consumer:

```json
{
  "peerDependencies": {
    "react": "^18.0.0"
  },
  "peerDependenciesMeta": {
    "react-dom": {
      "optional": true
    }
  }
}
```

npm 7+ auto-installs peer dependencies. When conflicts arise:

```bash
# See the dependency tree to diagnose
npm ls react

# Bypass peer dep conflicts (use cautiously)
npm install --legacy-peer-deps

# Force through all conflicts (last resort)
npm install --force
```

## Dependency Overrides

Force a specific version of a transitive dependency:

```json
{
  "overrides": {
    "lodash": "4.17.21",
    "express": {
      "qs": "6.11.0"
    }
  }
}
```

Overrides are useful for patching security vulnerabilities in transitive dependencies, resolving duplicate versions, and forcing compatible versions when npm resolution fails.

## npm audit

```bash
# Check for known vulnerabilities
npm audit

# Auto-fix vulnerabilities
npm audit fix

# Force major version updates to fix vulnerabilities
npm audit fix --force

# Get JSON output for programmatic use
npm audit --json

# Audit only production dependencies
npm audit --omit=dev

# Set audit level threshold
npm audit --audit-level=high
```

## node_modules Resolution

npm uses a flattened `node_modules` structure. When two packages require different versions of the same dependency, npm hoists the most common version and nests duplicates:

```
node_modules/
  express/           # v4.18.2 (hoisted)
  lodash/            # v4.17.21 (hoisted)
  some-package/
    node_modules/
      lodash/        # v3.10.1 (nested — needs older version)
```

**Phantom dependencies**: code can `require()` a hoisted package it does not declare. Always declare every import in your own `dependencies`.

sections.title: Install modes and flags
sections.body: Use `npm install <pkg>` to add production dependencies, `npm install -D <pkg>` for dev dependencies, `npm install -E <pkg>` for exact versions, and `npm install -g <pkg>` for global binaries. Combine flags freely: `npm install -DE typescript` pins an exact dev dependency. Use `--no-save` to install without modifying `package.json`. Use `--legacy-peer-deps` to ignore peer dependency conflicts when auto-resolution fails. Use `--omit=dev` to skip devDependencies in production builds.

sections.title: package-lock.json management
sections.body: The lock file pins every resolved dependency version and integrity hash. Always commit `package-lock.json` to version control. Never manually edit the lock file. Use `npm ci` in CI to install exactly what the lock file specifies — it fails if the lock file is out of sync with `package.json`. If lock file conflicts arise during merge, delete it and `node_modules`, then run `npm install` to regenerate. `npm install` updates the lock file when `package.json` changes; `npm ci` never modifies it.

sections.title: Peer dependency resolution
sections.body: Peer dependencies declare that a package expects the consumer to provide a specific dependency at a compatible version. Defined in `peerDependencies` in `package.json`. npm 7+ auto-installs peers, which can cause ERESOLVE conflicts when multiple packages require incompatible peer versions. Use `npm ls <pkg>` to trace the conflict tree. Use `--legacy-peer-deps` to fall back to npm 6 behavior (skip auto-install). Use `peerDependenciesMeta` to mark peers as optional. Use the `overrides` field to force a specific resolution when conflicts cannot be resolved otherwise.

sections.title: Security auditing with npm audit
sections.body: Run `npm audit` to scan installed packages against the npm advisory database. Use `npm audit fix` to automatically update vulnerable packages within semver ranges. Use `npm audit fix --force` to allow major version updates (may introduce breaking changes). Use `npm audit --omit=dev` to audit only production dependencies. Use `--audit-level=high` to set the minimum severity threshold. For transitive vulnerabilities that cannot be auto-fixed, use the `overrides` field in `package.json` to force a patched version.

Publishing requires dependencies to be installed and builds to pass
Scripts like postinstall run automatically after npm install

scripts.description: Install npm dependencies with optional flags for dev, exact, or CI mode and run an audit check

packages.description: Package names to install (empty array to install all from package.json)

auditSummary.description: Summary of npm audit results after installation

depends.reason: npm CLI must be available via the Node.js installation
